title: map — sorted key-value pairs
instruction:
`map<K, V>` stores key-value pairs sorted by key. O(log n) access.

```cpp
map<string, int> scores;
scores["Alice"] = 90;
scores.find("Bob");   // returns iterator
```

Use `[]` to access/insert, `.at()` for bounds-checked access.
successMessage: map iterates in alphabetical order by key. auto& [name, score] is structured binding — C++17, unpacks each pair. count() returns 1 if key exists, 0 otherwise.

title: unordered_map — O(1) hash map
instruction:
`unordered_map<K, V>` uses hashing — O(1) average access, faster than `map` for large datasets, but no ordering.

Use `unordered_map` when you don't need sorted keys.
hint: Same interface as map — just faster, unordered.
successMessage: wordCount[word]++ — if 'the' doesn't exist yet, [] creates it with 0, then ++ makes it 1. Second time it finds 1, increments to 2. Unordered: output order varies. Perfect for word frequency counting.

title: set and unordered_set — unique items
instruction:
`set<T>` stores unique sorted items. `unordered_set<T>` stores unique items with O(1) contains.

```cpp
set<int> s = {3, 1, 4, 1, 5};  // duplicate 1 is removed
// s = {1, 3, 4, 5} — sorted!
```
successMessage: set deduplicates and sorts automatically. set<string> from a vector removes duplicates in one line. Use set when you need both uniqueness and sorted order; unordered_set for faster membership testing.

title: stack, queue, and priority_queue
instruction:
Adaptor containers wrap other containers:

- `stack<T>` — LIFO (last in, first out)
- `queue<T>` — FIFO (first in, first out)
- `priority_queue<T>` — always access the largest element first
successMessage: Stack: 3 2 1 (LIFO). Queue: Alice Bob Carol (FIFO). Priority queue: 9 8 5 2 1 (largest first). priority_queue is a max-heap — used in Dijkstra's shortest path, event scheduling, and task priority systems.

title: pair and tuple
instruction:
`pair<T1, T2>` holds two values. `tuple<T1,T2,...>` holds any number.

```cpp
pair<string, int> p = {"Alice", 30};
p.first;   // "Alice"
p.second;  // 30
```

`make_pair` / `make_tuple` for construction.
successMessage: pairs are how map stores entries. Sorting a vector of pairs by second element with a lambda comparator — a very common pattern for leaderboards and rankings.

title: Build a frequency analyser
instruction:
No starter code.

Given this text: `"to be or not to be that is the question"`

1. Count word frequencies using `unordered_map<string, int>`
2. Find the top 3 most frequent words
3. Store all unique words in a `set<string>` and print them sorted
4. Print total word count and unique word count
hint: For top 3: copy to vector<pair<string,int>>, sort by .second descending, take first 3.

codeChecks.message: Use unordered_map<string, int> for word frequencies.
Use set<string> for unique sorted words.
Sort word frequencies to find top 3.
Parse the text into words (use stringstream or split on space).

successMessage: Frequency analysis using unordered_map + vector sort + set. Word frequency counting is the foundation of search engines, spam filters, and NLP. Chapter 16 done!
